questions.question: Select all that are true about the min-heap invariant and its consequences.
choices: Every parent node is at most each of its children.
The global minimum sits at the root and is readable in O(1).
Every element is fully sorted, so the whole array is in ascending order.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The invariant is a local promise that forces the minimum to the root, readable in O(1). Nothing is said about siblings, so the array is only partially ordered, not fully sorted.

questions.question: Select all that are true about how a heap node at index i maps to its neighbors in the backing array.
choices: Its children live at indices 2i+1 and 2i+2.
Its children live at indices i+1 and i+2.
Its parent lives at index i/2 rounded up.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Children live at 2i+1 and 2i+2 and the parent at (i-1) integer-divided by 2. The other two index formulas are wrong.

questions.question: Select all that are true about removing the minimum from a binary min-heap.
choices: You take the root as the answer, then move the last element into the root slot.
You sift the moved element down, swapping with its smaller child until the invariant holds.
Moving the last element to the root keeps the tree shape gap-free so the index formula stays valid.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Remove-min takes the root, promotes the last element, and sifts it down against the smaller child. Using the last element keeps the shape gap-free so the index arithmetic stays valid.

questions.question: Select all that are true about which heap to use to track the k LARGEST elements of a stream.
choices: A min-heap, so the smallest survivor sits at the root and is evicted first.
A max-heap, so the largest value sits at the root.
A sorted array rebuilt on every incoming element.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A min-heap keeps the weakest survivor at the root, ready to be evicted, so what remains is the k largest. A max-heap would guard the value you never want to drop.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the size-k min-heap trick for the k-th largest of n elements.
choices: The heap is never allowed to grow past size k, and the instant it exceeds k you pop the smallest.
The root of the size-k min-heap is exactly the k-th largest value.
It runs in O(n log k) time and O(k) space.
It runs in O(n log n) time because every element must be fully sorted.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The heap is capped at k, its root is the smallest survivor which is the k-th largest, and every operation touches a heap of size k for O(n log k) time and O(k) space. Nothing is fully sorted, so the last claim is false.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about using a max-heap for a 'k largest' question and about Python's heapq.
choices: A max-heap is correct because it keeps the largest value at the root, which is exactly the value you want to evict.
Python's heapq exposes a reverse or maxheap flag so it can behave as a max-heap directly.
For the k largest, a min-heap is correct because you want the weakest survivor on the chopping block.
To use heapq as a max-heap you push the negated value and negate again on the way out.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The largest value is the one you never want to evict, and heapq is min-only with no max flag, so the first two statements are false. The min-heap reasoning and the negate trick are true.

questions.question: Select all that are true about what a heap hands you for free.
choices: It returns all elements in fully sorted order with no extra work.
It guarantees the second-smallest element is always at a fixed known index.
It keeps sibling nodes ordered relative to each other.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A heap only makes the extreme cheap. It does not give a full sort, does not fix the second-smallest at a known index, and says nothing about sibling order, so None of the above is correct.

questions.question: Select all that are true about heap recognition cues and heap construction.
choices: 'k-th largest' is a classic heap fingerprint.
'merge k sorted lists' is a classic heap fingerprint.
Building a heap from n elements given all at once requires at least O(n log n).
None of the above
questions.explanation: 'k-th largest' and 'merge k sorted lists' are classic heap cues. Bottom-up heapify builds a heap from n elements in O(n), so the third claim is false.
